topics.title: JDBC & Connection Pooling
topics.description: JDBC (Java Database Connectivity) is the standard Java API for executing SQL. Core objects: DriverManager, Connection, PreparedStatement, ResultSet. Always use PreparedStatement over Statement to prevent SQL injection. Connection pools (HikariCP, c3p0) reuse connections because creating a new DB connection takes 50–100ms — far too expensive per request. HikariCP is the default pool in Spring Boot and the fastest available.
topics.story: Opening a raw JDBC connection is like hiring a contractor for every small task — interview, paperwork, setup, then fire them when done. HikariCP is like having a permanent team on standby: you borrow a worker (connection), they do the job, then go back to the pool ready for the next task. PreparedStatement is a pre-compiled SQL template — fill in the blanks with values. Statement is string-pasted SQL: 'WHERE id=' + userId — one missing quote and an attacker owns your database.
topics.analogy: Connection pool = taxi rank: cabs (connections) wait ready, you get in and out fast. DriverManager.getConnection() = hailing a cab from scratch every time (slow). PreparedStatement = printed form with blanks to fill in (safe). Statement = hand-written note (error-prone, injectable). ResultSet = a cursor moving through rows — next() advances it. try-with-resources = auto-return cab to the rank when done.
topics.memoryAnchor: Class.forName() not needed since JDBC 4.0 (auto-discovery). Connection conn = DriverManager.getConnection(url, user, pass). PreparedStatement ps = conn.prepareStatement(sql). ps.setString(1, value). ResultSet rs = ps.executeQuery(). rs.next(), rs.getString('col'). conn.setAutoCommit(false); conn.commit(); conn.rollback(). HikariCP: maximumPoolSize (default 10), connectionTimeout (30s), idleTimeout (10min). Spring Boot: spring.datasource.hikari.* properties.
topics.firstPrinciples: JDBC is a vendor-neutral API — MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle drivers implement the same interfaces. PreparedStatement sends SQL structure to the DB once, then sends only parameter values — parameters are always treated as data, never as SQL syntax, preventing injection. Connection pooling amortizes the TCP handshake + authentication cost across many requests. HikariCP uses a lock-free CAS-based pool and connection stealing to minimize contention.
topics.bruteForce: Create new Connection per request: 50–100ms overhead per DB call. String concatenation in SQL: 'WHERE name=\'' + input + '\'' — classic SQL injection vector. No transaction management: each statement auto-commits, no atomicity across related operations.
topics.optimizedApproach: HikariCP pool with sized pool (CPU*2 to CPU*4 for OLTP). PreparedStatement with ? placeholders. Explicit transactions for multi-statement operations. Batch updates (addBatch/executeBatch) for bulk inserts. ResultSet.TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY + CONCUR_READ_ONLY for large selects. Close resources in reverse order in finally or try-with-resources.
topics.whenToUse: JDBC directly: lightweight utilities, tools, batch jobs where Spring is overkill. Spring JdbcTemplate: removes boilerplate but keeps SQL control. JPA/Hibernate: domain-model-centric applications with complex object graphs. Raw JDBC for: stored procedures, bulk COPY operations, custom type mappings, or when you need fine-grained control over prepared statement reuse.

examples.title: JDBC CRUD with PreparedStatement & Transactions
examples.explanation: PreparedStatement separates SQL structure from values — the driver sends structure once, values separately, so user input can never alter the SQL syntax. RETURN_GENERATED_KEYS retrieves the auto-increment PK without a second query. Transactions: setAutoCommit(false) + commit() on success + rollback() on failure. The finally setAutoCommit(true) resets state before returning connection to pool.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring JdbcTemplate wraps this pattern (without the boilerplate). Spring @Transactional uses JDBC transactions under the hood. DevLearner: SeedBatchService uses JPA, which generates PreparedStatements automatically. Raw JDBC useful for bulk seed import via addBatch/executeBatch — 10x faster than individual inserts.

examples.title: HikariCP Connection Pool Configuration
examples.explanation: max-lifetime must be less than MySQL's wait_timeout (default 8 hours) to prevent 'connection closed' errors. connectionTimeout is how long the application waits for a connection from the pool — not the query timeout. addBatch() + executeBatch() sends all INSERTs in one network round-trip: 100 inserts take roughly the same time as 1. Spring Boot auto-configures HikariCP if it's on the classpath — you only need spring.datasource.* properties.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner uses Spring Boot's auto-configured HikariCP. Key tuning mistake: pool too large causes DB CPU thrashing (MySQL handles N connections with C threads — contention at C+). Kubernetes: each pod has its own pool — 10 pods × 10 connections = 100 DB connections. Monitor with actuator/metrics (hikaricp.connections.*). Set max-lifetime 5min less than MySQL wait_timeout.

problems.title: SQL Injection Prevention
problems.hint1: Use conn.prepareStatement("SELECT email FROM users WHERE name=?").
problems.hint2: ps.setString(1, name) — the driver escapes the value safely.
problems.hint3: With string concat, name=\"' OR '1'='1\" returns all users. PreparedStatement treats it as a literal string.
problems.testCases:
Injection input: ' OR '1'='1
PreparedStatement: treated as literal string, no rows match
Safe: no injection possible

problems.title: JDBC Batch Insert
problems.description: Implement `batchInsert(Connection conn, List<String> tags)` that inserts all tags into a `tags(name VARCHAR)` table using a single batch (not N separate inserts). Print the number of rows inserted.
problems.hint1: Use prepareStatement, then loop calling addBatch() for each item.
problems.hint2: After the loop, call executeBatch() — returns int[] with update counts.
problems.hint3: Wrap in a transaction (setAutoCommit false) for atomicity — all or nothing.

problems.title: Connection Pool Sizing Calculation
problems.description:
Given: a Spring Boot app with 4 CPU cores, average query time of 5ms, expected 500 req/sec peak. Answer:
1. What is the optimal HikariCP pool size? (use formula: pool size = (core_count * 2) + effective_spindle_count)
2. What is the maximum throughput with that pool size (connections * 1000ms/5ms)?
3. What spring.datasource.hikari.* properties would you set?
problems.hint1: HikariCP formula: pool = (cores * 2) + 1. For SSD/cloud DB, spindle count = 0.
problems.hint2: Throughput = pool_size * (1000ms / avg_query_ms). Compare with 500 req/sec target.
problems.hint3: Set maximum-pool-size, connection-timeout (don't let it queue too long), and max-lifetime (< MySQL wait_timeout).
problems.testCases:
Pool size: 9
Max RPS: 1800.0
Sufficient for 500 rps? true
spring.datasource.hikari.maximum-pool-size=9
spring.datasource.hikari.connection-timeout=5000
spring.datasource.hikari.max-lifetime=1800000
